title: Everglades
summary: The wetland where water, fire, and slow time first became one system.
description: A landscape of wet seasons, fire scars, and patient water that first taught me to read ecology as layered process instead of isolated event.

title: Beijing
summary: Where scientific training sharpened curiosity into method.
description: In Beijing, field observation began to connect with formal ecological thinking, and scattered questions slowly took the shape of a discipline.

title: Guizhou
summary: Karst valleys and damaged mountains made development and ecology impossible to separate.
description: A journey through Guizhou revealed how environmental questions are rarely only scientific; they are also political, infrastructural, and deeply human.

title: Qinghai
summary: At altitude, beauty and extraction occupied the same valley.
description: Qinghai made visible the uneasy coexistence of ecological policy, industrial ambition, and mountain landscapes still carrying their own dignity.

title: California
summary: Where ecology, computation, and public service began to align.
description: California widened the frame, showing how environmental thinking can travel into data, institutions, and the design of public systems.

title: Hainan
summary: A tropical island where ecology showed a more humid, ocean-facing rhythm.
description: Hainan opened another climatic register: one where tropical systems, coastlines, and atmospheric change reframed what a landscape can teach.

title: Inner Mongolia
summary: A field investigation that turned ecological accounting into a question of responsibility.
description: In Inner Mongolia, numbers stopped feeling neutral. The journey exposed how scientific analysis can become evidence, consequence, and moral weight all at once.
